source.title: Is the rostro-caudal axis of the frontal lobe hierarchical?

confidence_rationale: Foundational anchor for R3 (hierarchy depth verdict). Badre & D'Esposito review anatomical, neuroimaging, electrophysiological and developmental findings supporting a multi-level functional hierarchy along the rostro-caudal axis of frontal cortex. Anterior regions encode more abstract action representations; posterior regions encode more concrete motor specifications. The hierarchical decomposition direction (abstract intention -> concrete motor act) is exactly the inverse of REE's chunk-to-primitive composition direction, so ARC-070's decomposition direction is grounded in the same anatomical substrate. R3 verdict (multi-level vs single-level decomposition) is settled in favour of multi-level by this paper's evidence: the brain instantiates AT LEAST 3-4 levels of action abstraction along the rostro-caudal axis, and the corresponding mechanism shape for ARC-070 is multi-level chunk -> sub-chunk -> primitive recursion, not single-level chunk-to-primitive only.
failure_signatures: An ARC-070 child-MECH that supports only single-level decomposition (chunk -> primitive, with no chunk-of-chunks recursion) will fail to match the multi-level hierarchical organisation Badre & D'Esposito document in frontal cortex. The mechanism design must support recursive decomposition with practical recursion bounds.

If the implementation places all decomposition operations at a single anatomical / computational locus (no functional differentiation across abstraction levels), the design will lose the predictive structure of the rostro-caudal gradient and any rule-apprehension layer (ARC-062) reading the decomposed sequence will lose the abstraction-level signal that the hierarchy carries.

mapping.source_claim_statement: The frontal cortex shows a rostro-caudal gradient in functional activity scaled by the abstractness of action representations: more anterior regions (frontopolar, anterior PFC) encode more abstract action goals and rules; more posterior regions (lateral PFC, premotor cortex) encode progressively more concrete stimulus-response associations and motor specifications. Hierarchical control flows along this axis: anterior regions influence posterior regions during the realisation of abstract goals as motor acts. The hierarchy involves multiple levels (at minimum: response, feature, dimension, context / task-set, episode), with empirical evidence from anatomy, fMRI, single-unit recording, and developmental studies.
mapping.ree_translation: ARC-070's decomposition direction is anatomically supported as a multi-level hierarchy. The chunk-to-primitive zoom-in is one step in a longer chain: most-abstract intention -> task-set -> action sequence (chunked primitive) -> individual action -> motor specification. ARC-070's child-MECH design should permit recursion: a chunked primitive can decompose into sub-chunks, each of which can decompose further, with the recursion depth bounded by the depth of the predictive model that licenses each abstraction level. The R3 verdict (multi-level vs single-level) settles in favour of multi-level. Practical implementation may bound the recursion depth at 2-3 levels for tractability, but the architectural commitment is recursive in principle.
mapping.mapping_caveat: Badre & D'Esposito's evidence is on EXECUTIVE control of overt action, not on rollout-side imagination. The rostro-caudal gradient is documented during task-set selection, action planning, and rule application; the imagination-side application (ARC-070's domain) is a theoretical extension. The transfer is licensed by the broader episodic-simulation literature (Schacter 2008 companion entry) showing imagination shares network substrate with action / memory, but the specific rostro-caudal evidence does not directly come from imagination experiments. Additionally, the 2009 review notes ongoing controversy about whether the gradient is strictly hierarchical or rather a graded specialisation; the synthesis recommends hierarchical interpretation as the working hypothesis with sensitivity to the disconfirming literature.
mapping.source_context: Foundational Nature Reviews Neuroscience review, integrating anatomical, fMRI, single-unit, and developmental evidence on frontal-lobe organisation. Authors are leading figures in cognitive control / prefrontal organisation.

confidence_components.notes: Source quality high (NRN review, well cited, integrates multiple methodologies). Mapping fidelity moderate-strong on R3 (multi-level hierarchy directly supported), weaker on ARC-070's specific imagination-side application (the rostro-caudal evidence is mostly about overt action). Transfer risk moderate-high because the mapping from frontal-cortex action hierarchy to REE's rollout-side decomposition runs through episodic-simulation theory, not direct empirical evidence.
